title: Salmon en Croute
subtitle: A whole salmon fillet wrapped in puff pastry with spinach and dill, baked until golden
excerpt: Salmon en croute is a whole salmon fillet sandwiched with wilted spinach, dill, and cream cheese inside a sealed puff pastry case. It looks impressive, bakes in 25 minutes, and holds well for 15 minutes after coming out of the oven.
sourceNotes: French en croute technique applied to British salmon. Delia Smith, Complete Cookery Course (1978): salmon in pastry method. Elizabeth David, French Provincial Cooking (1960): en croute principles. British dinner party staple from the 1970s onwards.

content.text: is one of those dishes that looks far harder than it is. The pastry is shop-bought and ready-rolled; the filling is wilted spinach and cream cheese spread onto the salmon. The whole thing wraps around the fish in five minutes and bakes in 25. The main risk is overcooking: salmon needs to reach only 55-58°C at the centre for a translucent, moist result.
is applied just before baking. The pastry must be cold when it goes in; a warm parcel will give a greasy, flat result.

items.name: Salmon fillet
items.prepNote: approx. 600 g total, skin removed, even thickness

content.text: in a dry frying pan for 2 minutes, then tip onto a clean cloth and squeeze out as much moisture as possible. Chop roughly. Mix with the cream cheese, most of the
. Taste; the mixture should be well seasoned.

Lay one sheet of pastry on a lightly floured surface. Place one salmon fillet in the centre. Spread the spinach mixture over the top, then lay the second salmon fillet on top to form a sandwich. Lay the second pastry sheet over the top, tucking it under the base sheet all around. Trim the edges to 2 cm, then seal by crimping with a fork. Transfer to a baking tray lined with baking paper. Chill for 30 minutes.

Heat the oven to 220°C (200°C fan). Score the surface of the pastry lightly in a diagonal pattern, being careful not to cut through to the filling. Brush all over with beaten egg. Bake for 22-25 minutes until deeply golden and puffed. Rest for 10 minutes before slicing with a sharp knife.

rows.fix: Squeeze the cooked spinach very hard in a clean cloth or several layers of kitchen paper. The filling must be dry before it touches the pastry.
rows.cause: Spinach was not squeezed dry enough, releasing moisture during baking

rows.fix: 220°C is deliberate. High heat cooks the pastry quickly before the salmon has time to overcook. Probe through the side: 55°C is translucent and moist, 60°C is fully opaque.
rows.cause: Baked at too low a temperature, extending the time in the oven
rows.symptom: Salmon is overcooked and dry inside

rows.fix: Dampen the seam edges and crimp firmly. Make sure there is a 2 cm border of plain pastry all around.
rows.cause: Seam was not sealed well enough, or the filling was overstuffed
rows.symptom: Pastry bursts open at the seam

rows.fix: Chill the assembled parcel for at least 30 minutes. Cold pastry rises higher and crisps better.
rows.cause: Parcel was warm when it went into the oven, or pastry was warm
rows.symptom: The parcel is flat and unimpressive after baking

content.text: Salmon en croute was a 1970s dinner party staple in Britain. A generation of cooks made it fashionable as they explored French technique with British ingredients. It has never quite left the repertoire because it delivers: a golden parcel that slices cleanly, holds its shape, and needs nothing more than a cucumber salad or plain vegetables alongside.
